mnemonics.title: UX Design Process
mnemonics.content: UX process: E-DI-P-T (Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test)

mnemonics.title: Nielsen's 10 Heuristics
mnemonics.content: Heuristics: VISUAL (Visibility, In-real-world match, User control, Consistency, Error prevention, Aesthetic design, Learnability) - think of system showing status clearly, speaking user's language, letting user undo, staying consistent, preventing errors, looking good, helping users learn

mnemonics.title: SE vs UX Focus
mnemonics.content: SE focuses on BUILD (Build reliably, Use efficient algorithms, Maintainability, Debug quickly); UX focuses on FEEL (Feel satisfied, Enjoyable, Easy to use, Fulfills needs)

mnemonics.title: Agile Values
mnemonics.content: Agile: I-P-F-C (Individuals over processes, Working software over docs, Customer collaboration over contracts, Responding to change over following plan)

acronyms.usage: Overall experience a user has while interacting with a software product, including satisfaction, ease of use, and emotional response
The visual and interactive elements (buttons, menus, screens) through which users interact with the software
Design approach that prioritizes user needs, preferences, and limitations throughout the development process
Structural design of content organization, navigation, and labeling to help users find information easily
Standardized 10-question questionnaire for measuring perceived usability of a system
Disciplined approach to designing, developing, testing, and maintaining software systems
Product version with just enough features to satisfy early customers and provide feedback for future development
Software development methodology emphasizing rapid prototyping and quick delivery
Standard paradigm for graphical user interfaces

visualTips.visualization: Draw two overlapping circles - left circle labeled 'Software Engineering' with technical keywords (code, architecture, algorithms), right circle labeled 'UX' with user keywords (satisfaction, ease, emotions). The overlap represents areas requiring collaboration - this is where successful products exist

Draw a circular arrow cycle: Prototype → Test → Analyze → Refine → Prototype. Show each phase feeding into the next continuously, emphasizing that this never truly ends - it just moves to different feature areas

Draw a 2-week horizontal timeline: Day 1 Planning → Daily Standups (small vertical marks) → Development work → Mid-sprint review → Final Demo → Retrospective. Show UX tasks happening throughout, not just at beginning

Draw two diamonds side by side: First diamond (widen narrow): Divergent research → Convergent Define. Second diamond (widen narrow): Divergent Ideate → Convergent Prototype/Test. Shows how design alternates between exploring and narrowing

Draw a pyramid with 10 blocks: Base (visibility of system status, match with real world, user control), Middle (consistency, error prevention, recognition), Top (flexibility, aesthetics, error recovery, help). Shows foundation to advanced usability principles

analogies.analogy: Building a restaurant: SE is the kitchen infrastructure (ovens, refrigeration, plumbing) that must work efficiently - UX is the dining experience (ambiance, menu layout, waiter service) that makes customers enjoy their meal. Both are essential; poor kitchen ruins dining experience, poor service ruins even good food.

Sculpting a statue from marble - you don't carve the final form in one attempt. Each chisel reveals more of the form, you step back to assess, make corrections. Final masterpiece emerges through many iterations.
Building a custom suit - you measure the person first, understand how they move, what they need pockets for, then design and tailor. Not one-size-fits-all approach.

Architectural blueprints and model buildings before construction. Just as architects test if a design works with a small model before expensive construction, UX prototypes test if design works before expensive development.
Method acting - to play a character authentically, actors deeply understand what their character thinks, feels, says, and does in different situations. Designers do the same for users.

Library organization system - just as libraries categorize books (fiction, non-fiction, by author, subject) to help patrons find what they need, IA organizes digital content so users can navigate easily.
